items.correct: Attributing human emotions or qualities to nature or inanimate objects to reflect a character's feelings or scene's mood (e.g. angry storms during conflict).
items.misconception: Pathetic fallacy is when an argument in a story is weak or sad.
items.random_wrong: Pathetic fallacy is when characters cry over silly things.
items.why: The term uses 'pathetic' in its old sense — pertaining to emotion (pathos) — not 'feeble'. Students misread it as a value judgement when it is a technical device coined by Ruskin.

items.q: What did Romantic poets value in nature?
items.correct: Nature as a source of spiritual insight and emotional truth, accessed through imagination — not picturesque scenery for its own sake.
items.misconception: Romantic poets just liked to write about pretty landscapes.
items.random_wrong: Romantic poets believed nature was full of fairies and elves.
items.why: The Romantic engagement with nature was philosophical — it was a route to truth, the sublime and the self. Reducing it to 'pretty scenery' misses Wordsworth's epistemology entirely.

items.correct: Experimentation with form (fragmentation, stream of consciousness), focus on subjective interior experience, scepticism toward traditional narrative and absolute truth.
items.misconception: Modernism just means literature written in modern times.
items.random_wrong: Modernism is literature about modern technology like phones.
items.why: Modernism is a specific early 20th-century movement (Joyce, Woolf, Eliot), not a synonym for contemporary. Students use it as a vague chronological term, missing its formal experimentation.

items.q: Who coined the term unreliable narrator in 1961?
items.why: Correct answer: Wayne Booth. “Northrop Frye” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which Marquez novel exemplifies magic realism?
items.misconception: Love in the Time of Cholera
items.why: It's One Hundred Years of Solitude. “Love in the Time of Cholera” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Marquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude published?
items.why: The real figure is 1967 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
